EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another swell from a strong low far northwest of the islands caused surf of 15 to 30 feet along the north-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, and Molokai; and 6 to 10 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii.  The rough surf kept lifeguards busy with rescues, other assistance such as warnings, and preventive actions.  There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the Aloha State generated surf of 25 to 50 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 15 to 30 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai.  The dangerous surf again kept ocean safety officials busy with rescues, assists, warnings, and preventive actions.  However, no serious injuries were reported.  Water from the high surf did enter two beachfront properties in Pupukea on the North Shore of Oahu on the 27th.  Also on Oahu, water was reported on the roadway by the 7 Eleven in Makaha and within the parking lot at Haleiwa Beach Park.  Any costs of damages were not available.

Very strong north to northwest winds developed and reached warning level wind speeds in the early morning hours of January 9th around 2 AM HST.  Sustained winds were measured by instruments as high as 64 mph, with maximum gusts reaching 80 mph.  The winds diminished in the afternoon of the 9th, dropping below warning levels at about 2 PM HST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained reach 66 mph at 4 AM HST.  Sustained remained at 55 to 65 mph for nearly 12 hours.  Maximum wind gust recorded was 80 mph at 3:59 AM HST.

A fire blackened nearly 200 acres of mainly fountaingrass near Hawaii Community College-Palamanui in leeward Big Island.  No structures were threatened, though some nearby residents and people at the college voluntarily evacuated the area because of the smoke.  There were no reports of significant injuries.  The cause of the blaze was under investigation.

A fire scorched about 200 acres of brush in Maalaea on Maui.   No structures were threatened by the flames.  And there were no reports of significant injuries.  The blaze began around a quarter mile south of Maalaea Harbor, though the cause was unknown.

The high swell and surf did complicate the search for possible survivors of two Marine Corps helicopters that had gone down two miles north of Haleiwa on the North Shore of Oahu late Thursday, Jan. 14.  The Marine Corps and Coast Guard later declared all 12 men aboard the helicopters dead after several days of searching.

A fast-moving cold front and building high pressure brought gusty north to northeast winds to Oahu and around the Aloha State.  Power outages, and downed trees and power lines were common across the island chain.||On Oahu, a downed power line on the morning of the 9th prompted closure of McArthur Street, which then blocked access to Waianae Elementary School on the isle's leeward coast.  Downed trees in the evening of the 8th and early hours of the 9th were reported in Waipahu, Kunia, Wahiawa/Schofield area, Hawaii Kai, Waialae Iki Neighborhood Park, along Kalanianaole Highway, and in Mililani District Park.  And at the Koko Head Shooting Complex, a person was injured when gusty winds took the roof off a structure and flipped it over.  The cost of damages was not available.

A fire blackened around 2500 acres of mainly brush near Nanakuli in leeward Oahu, though a few endangered plant species were destroyed in the area.  The cause of the blaze was unknown.  No serious injuries were reported.  The cost of the damages to the endangered plants were not available.

A blaze charred around 200 acres of brush in Range 3 of the Army's Pohakuloa Training Area (PTA) on the Big Island.  The fire was allowed to burn itself out because it was surrounded by lava and a road network that served as barriers to stop the blaze from spreading.  Also, the closest vegetation that would sustain the fire was more than one mile away.  No significant property damage or injuries were reported.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the islands generated surf of 15 to 30 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and along the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 25 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai; and 8 to 15 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island.  The dangerous surf kept lifeguards busy will warnings and assisting beach-goers that were in distress.  The surf also forced officials to close several beaches in leeward Big Island for safety reasons on the 31st.  There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

Swells from lows north and northwest of the Aloha State generated surf of 15 to 30 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai; and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island; and 8 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai.  The dangerous surf kept lifeguards busy with providing warnings and assisting individuals in distress.  The high water also deposited debris on surface roads near the shores on each isle, with a roadway near Hawaiian Beaches subdivision on the Big Island being especially hard-hit with plenty of rocks and sand on the 19th.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.

A period of light winds with plenty of low-level moisture and transiting upper disturbances produced heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms over much of the Aloha State.  There were also flash flood events as time went along.  No serious injuries were reported.||The costs of any damages from the heavy downpours were not available, including damages to a barge, the Wade K, that went aground at the Wailua Marina on Kauai in the early morning hours of the 25th (after the Wailua River had flooded).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than a dozen hikers were stranded for a time because of high water levels in the Makamakaole Gulch.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike started a wildfire that consumed nearly 1800 acres of mainly brush around Puuanahulu along Highway 190 in North Kona on the lee side of the Big Island.  Most of the fire was extinguished or contained by 1500 HST on the 24th.  There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

Although there were periods of heavy rain and flash flooding over portions of Maui and the Big Island in March, drought conditions persisted or got worse over other parts of these isles, with these areas seeing severe drought, or the D2 category in the Drought Monitor.

Swells from lows north and northwest of the Aloha State generated surf of 15 to 30 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai; and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island; and 8 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai.  The dangerous surf kept lifeguards busy with providing warnings and assisting individuals in distress.  The high water also deposited debris on surface roads near the shores on each isle, with a roadway near Hawaiian Beaches subdivision on the Big Island being especially hard-hit with plenty of rocks and sand on the 19th.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.

Strong West-Northwest winds were measured at the summit of the Big Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 55 to 65 mph for the duration of the event.  Maximum gusts reached 74 mph.

No serious injuries were reported.||The costs of any damages from the heavy downpours were not available, including damages to a barge, the Wade K, that went aground at the Wailua Marina on Kauai in the early morning hours of the 25th (after the Wailua River had flooded).

Swells from lows north and northwest of the Aloha State generated surf of 15 to 30 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai; and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island; and 8 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai.  The dangerous surf kept lifeguards busy with providing warnings and assisting individuals in distress.  The high water also deposited debris on surface roads near the shores on each isle, with a roadway near Hawaiian Beaches subdivision on the Big Island being especially hard-hit with plenty of rocks and sand on the 19th.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maui Civil Defense reported a swift water rescue of a family of eight in Halawa Valley at the east end of Molokai.  Another party of 12 was also located and helped to leave the area.  The groups had been trapped by high water rising in a stream and waited for the water to subside before exiting the valley.  When firefighters arrived on the scene, everyone had already successfully crossed the stream.

In advance of an approaching front, areas of showers developed over northern parts of Kauai and in the North Kona District on the lee side of the Big Island of Hawaii.  The precipitation produced small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.  ||However, the rains on Kauai swelled the Hanakapiai Stream enough to make it impassable.  More than 100 hikers along a trail in the area were trapped and had to be rescued by air crews with the Kauai Fire Department over a two-day period, 3-4 April.

With daytime heating, heavy showers developed over parts of East and West Maui.  The rain produced small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

A weakening front and an upper trough combined to generate heavy showers over parts of Kauai, Oahu, the Big Island of Hawaii, and, most particularly, Maui.  The rain caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  Flash flooding occurred on Maui, in and around Hana, where roads were closed for a time because of high water.  There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

Very strong winds developed over the Big Island Summits, reaching high wind warning levels by 11:30 pm HST on the 6th, and peaking out between 10 am HST on the 7th and 3 pm HST on the 7th.  During the peak, wind gusts reached 114 mph at the CFHT observing station on Mauna Kea summit.  Sustained winds reached up to 80 mph during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CFHT-GEM telescope wind instrumentation on Mauna Kea measured peak wind gusts of 114 mph at 10 AM HST and 3 PM HST on Feb. 7th.

Another blaze in leeward West Maui charred 1300 acres of brush, not far from the fire in the previous week.  The fire was located in the Olowalu-Ukumehame area, and not related to the other blaze.  The fire did come close to one residence, but it did not  damage the structure in the end.  There were no reports of significant injuries, or property damage otherwise.  The cause of the fire was not known.

A northeast swell caused by former Hurricane Celia generated surf of 6 to 12 feet along the east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island of Hawaii.  The initial surge of energy produced rough conditions at Oahu's Makapuu Tidepools on the 16th, which resulted in the death of a father and daughter.  There were no reports of serious property damage.

A fire charred about 400 acres of brush near Waikii Ranch and the Army's Pohakuloa Training Area on the Big Island of Hawaii.  The blaze did not damage any structures or cause harm to native vegetation, and there were no reports of injuries.  The cause of the fire was under investigation.

A fire blackened around 200 acres of brush in Pukalani Terrace on the Valley Isle of Maui.  The blaze forced the evacuation of about a dozen homes on the south and west sides of Akalani Loop but did not cause any damage to residences.  No serious injuries were reported.  The origin of the fire was undetermined.

With the remnant circulation of former Tropical Cyclone Celia passing north of the islands; a humid, light-wind regime became momentarily established over the Aloha State.  The result was heavy downpours over portions of Oahu, the Big Island, and Maui; bringing ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  However, no significant property damage or injuries were reported.

As remnants of Tropical Cyclone Orlene moved north of the island chain, moisture to the south and southeast of the feature produced heavy showers over upslope portions of East Maui, and also contributed to evening convection in leeward portions of the Big Island of Hawaii.  The rain caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

As a weak tropical disturbance with abundant low-level moisture moved through the islands, an upper low moved in from the northwest.  This combination generated heavy showers and thunderstorms, which then resulted in flash flooding over Maui, especially in the Iao Valley.  Other parts of the state received heavy rainfall as well.  An initial estimate of damages was about $15 million; though this was, apparently, for public costs.  No estimate was available for private losses.  No serious injuries were reported, though several residents from Iao Valley had to be rescued because of flood waters.

A swell from Tropical Cyclone Madeline generated surf of 8 to 25 feet along the east-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii, Maui, Molokai, Oahu, and Kauai, with the highest surf over eastern parts of the island chain.  The system also produced gusty winds and heavy showers over parts of the Big Island.  Power outages and minor flooding were the result.  No significant property damage or injuries were reported, however. This episode is continued from the end of August.

As Hurricane Lester passed to the north of the Aloha State, its swell train boosted surf on east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island of Hawaii.  Surf heights reached 10 to 20 feet.  Lifeguards were busy with providing advice to beach-goers and rescuing those who encountered problems in the water. The rain produced small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  No significant injuries or property damage were reported.

Tropical Storm Darby affected the island chain with high surf, gusty winds, and heavy downpours; from the Big Island to Kauai.  Some of the precipitation resulted in flash flooding.  There were power outages, and downed trees and power poles in parts of the Big Island and Oahu.  However, there were no reports of significant injuries.  The cost of damages was not available.||Surf heights varied around the island chain, though affected east-facing shores were on Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island of Hawaii. Most surf was in the 8 to 12-foot range, with some Big Island beaches seeing heights as high as 20 feet on the 22nd and 23rd.

An unscheduled fire on the west side of Haliimaile on Maui scorched around 200 acres of sugar cane.  The blaze did not threaten any structures or vehicles, and no injuries were reported.  The cost of the cane losses was not available.  The cause of the fire was under investigation.

A swell from a strong low far northwest of the islands produced surf of 10 to 18 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 8 to 14 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.

Large swells from the northwest generated surf of 20 to 40 feet, with sets as high as 55 feet, on the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 15 to 25 feet, with sets as high as 35 feet, on the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai, and the north-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii; and 8 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of Maui and the Big Island.  ||The large surf caused beach erosion and damage to roadways in some cases, inundated parking areas in coastal recreation areas, and closed beaches.  One man was swept out to sea as a large wave broke where he was taking pictures at Queen's Bath on Kauai.  The cost of damages was not available.  No other significant injuries were reported.

Several fires in leeward parts of the Big Island of Hawaii scorched more than 1100 acres of dry brush and fountain grass.  The fires were located near and along major roadways, especially in the vicinity of the Daniel K. Inouye Highway and Highway 190 between Kailua-Kona and Waimea.  The blazes disrupted traffic as roads had to be closed occasionally because of the fires' danger to motorists.  The incidents were being treated as cases of arson.  No homes or other structures were threatened.  No serious injuries were reported.

A fire that blackened about 4700 acres in the West Maui Mountains started near the intersection of Kuihelani and Honoapiilani highways.  Hikers in the area and a few firefighters were almost lost as the flames cut them off from safety for a time (on the 2nd of July).  The blaze, at its worst, raged over the area with winds at 25 to 30 mph.  The fire burned from near Maalaea to Ukumehame on the south side of Central and West Maui.  No significant injuries were reported.  The cost of damages was not available, and the cause of the blaze was undetermined.

A series of swells from the northwest produced surf of 15 to 30 feet, with some sets as high as 50 feet, along the north-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 20 feet, with occasional sets to 35 feet, along the west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, and Molokai; and 6 to 10 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big island of Hawaii.  ||The surf periodically inundated portions of roadways on Oahu's North Shore, along Pupukea's Ke Waena Road for instance, and in the windward area, with some surfaces sustaining damage.  The most significant problem occurred near Kaaawa along Kamehameha Highway, where rough surf washed out the northbound lane.  The cost of the damages was not available.  No serious injuries were reported.

Tropical Storm Darby affected the island chain with high surf, gusty winds, and heavy downpours; from the Big Island to Kauai.  Some of the precipitation resulted in flash flooding.  There were power outages, and downed trees and power poles in parts of the Big Island and Oahu.  However, there were no reports of significant injuries.  The cost of damages was not available.||Surf heights varied around the island chain, though affected east-facing shores were on Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island of Hawaii. Most surf was in the 8 to 12-foot range, with some Big Island beaches seeing heights as high as 20 feet on the 22nd and 23rd.

Strong winds caused power outages, downed trees, and blew roofs off structures in parts of Oahu, including Manoa, Aina Haina, Kalihi, and Nuuanu.  The winds were associated with strong high pressure centered far northwest of the Aloha State.  The cost of the damages was not available.  One man was slightly injured in Nuuanu when a tree fell on his home and trapped him in his bed.  No other injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a home and pinned a man in his bed in Nuuanu.  The man refused treatment by paramedics.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fire blackened about 5300 acres of dry brush, grass, and haole koa in the southern parts of East Maui.  The blaze was along and on either side of Piilani Highway between mile markers 22 and 27, which forced the closure of the roadway for periods of time while firefighters worked to get the fire under control.  The blaze also caused 10 residents to flee the Kahikinui Homesteads as a precaution.||The fire was contained by Saturday, Feb. 20, but it was not extinguished because much of it was inaccessible.  Another flare-up occurred on the 24th, and the fire began moving up the slope of Haleakala.  It remained inaccessible in places, so fire officials planned to let the blaze burn itself out.  The cause of the fire was not known.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

Heavy showers over northern parts of Kauai raised stream levels and trapped hikers along the Kalalau Trail in the Napali Coast State Wilderness Park.  Hanakapiai Stream flooded and forced about a dozen individuals to spend a night stranded near the waterway.  One couple had almost been swept away when they unsuccessfully attempted to cross the swollen stream on a makeshift line.  However, no significant property damage or injuries were reported.||On Maui, heavy downpours flooded the Maui County Service Center at the Maui Mall in Kahului.  The rain damaged computers and motor vehicle records, and shut down power.  The cost of damages was not available.  There no serious injuries.

A series of swells from the northwest produced surf of 15 to 30 feet, with some sets as high as 50 feet, along the north-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 20 feet, with occasional sets to 35 feet, along the west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, and Molokai; and 6 to 10 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big island of Hawaii.  ||The surf periodically inundated portions of roadways on Oahu's North Shore, along Pupukea's Ke Waena Road for instance, and in the windward area, with some surfaces sustaining damage.  The most significant problem occurred near Kaaawa along Kamehameha Highway, where rough surf washed out the northbound lane.  The cost of the damages was not available.  No serious injuries were reported.

A swell from former Tropical Cyclone Howard caused surf of 6 to 10 feet along the east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island of Hawaii.  The area of lower pressure moving through the islands also provided abundant moisture for heavy rain events over Oahu and Maui. However, there were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.

A mid-level trough enhanced showers over parts of Oahu.  The rain produced ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.

Trade showers sweeping in from the east generated small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways, in east and southeast sections of the Big Island of Hawaii.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

An upper trough, developing into a low, north of the Aloha State helped induce heavy showers over portions of leeward Big island.  The rain produced small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

An unscheduled sugar cane fire charred between 150 and 200 acres near the intersection of Omaopio and Pulehu roads on the Valley Isle of Maui.  The blaze was in fields owned by Hawaiian Commercial & Sugar Company (HC&S).  The fire did not threaten homes or any other structures.  The cost of damages was not available.  There were no injuries reported.  The blaze may have begun as a flare-up from a previous fire that had occurred a day before.

A swell from Tropical Cyclone Madeline generated surf of 8 to 25 feet along the east-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii, Maui, Molokai, Oahu, and Kauai, with the highest surf over eastern parts of the island chain.  The system also produced gusty winds and heavy showers over parts of the Big Island.  Power outages and minor flooding were the result.  No significant property damage or injuries were reported, however.

The combination of upper-level instability and tropical moisture, some of which were remnants from former Tropical Storm Ulika, generated heavy rain and isolated thunderstorms over the parts of the Big Island of Hawaii, Maui, and Oahu.  The precipitation produced small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  However, on the Big Island in the evening of the 4th and the morning of the 5th, the almost non-stop rain caused some areas around Hilo Bay to be submerged under 2 feet of water.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

However, on the Big Island in the evening of the 4th and the morning of the 5th, the almost non-stop rain caused some areas around Hilo Bay to be submerged under 2 feet of water.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

A fire charred 90 acres of brush and grass near Kahikinui and Kaupo on the Valley Isle of Maui.  The blaze did not threaten any structures or vehicles, and no serious injuries were reported.  Its cause was under investigation.

An upper low and a separate upper trough generated heavy rain and showers, isolated thunderstorms, and flash flooding over much of the Aloha State.  The features also produced snow and blustery weather to the upper elevations of the Big Island of Hawaii.||A woman was swept away and killed during flash flooding on Kauai on the 3rd during a kayak and hiking tour near the Wailua River.  ||An aircraft with Hawaiian Airlines suffered relatively minor damage from a lightning strike as it was inbound to the Hilo airport on the Big Island of Hawaii on the 1st.  The costs of any other damages that may have occurred were not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pilot for Hawaiian Airlines reported a lightning strike on his aircraft inbound to Hilo on the Big Island of Hawaii.

A moderate, short-period, north-northeast swell caused surf of 6 to 10 feet along the east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big island of Hawaii.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries, though a portion of Bayfront Highway along Hilo Bay was closed for a time because of the high surf and debris on the roadway, and a partial closure of a parking area at Leleiwi Park because of waves flowing onto the pavement.  Both closures were on the 14th.

A surface low, or kona low in Hawaiian parlance, developed north of the state and produced heavy showers over parts of Kauai and the Big Island of Hawaii.  The rain caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

A surface low, or kona low in Hawaiian parlance, developed north of the state and produced heavy showers over parts of Kauai and the Big Island of Hawaii.  The rain caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

A swell from a strong low far northwest of the Aloha State generated surf of 15 to 25 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, and Molokai; and the north-facing shores of Oahu and Maui; 10 to 20 feet along the north-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii; and 10 to 15 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu.  The high water inundated parking areas at Baldwin Beach Park on Maui on the 3rd, and officials closed the area for a time until the higher surf subsided.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

Very dry conditions persisted across portions of the Aloha State in November.  As a matter of fact, parts of leeward Kauai and Maui deteriorated from the D2 category of severe drought to the D3 level of extreme drought.

A fire blackened approximately 2000 acres of mainly dry brush in leeward Oahu, in the Waianae Valley.  The blaze did engulf between 400 and 500 vehicles at a towing and auto parts business in the area, though the cost of damages had not been determined.  And the fire for a time had threatened homes and other structures but was turned away by winds and the actions of the firefighters.  No significant injuries were reported.  The cause of the blaze was not known.

A slow-moving front stalled over the Valley Isle of Maui and brought heavy rain and showers.  The system produced flash flooding conditions in windward Maui on the 31st. No serious injuries were reported.  The costs of any damages were not available.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mauna Kea rangers closed road at the Visitor Center due to snow and ice and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds developed across the high elevations of the Big Island around Mauna Kea early on the 11th and continued through the early morning hours of the 12th.  Peak wind gusts reached 81 mph during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 60 to 75 mph for the duration of this event at the CFHT anemometer on Mauna Kea summit.

A swell from a strong low far northwest of the Aloha State generated surf of 15 to 25 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, and Molokai; and the north-facing shores of Oahu and Maui; 10 to 20 feet along the north-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii; and 10 to 15 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu.  The high water inundated parking areas at Baldwin Beach Park on Maui on the 3rd, and officials closed the area for a time until the higher surf subsided.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.
